era_context: The early 19th century, marked by industrial expansion in Europe and North America, nationalist and liberal revolutions, and the consolidation of European colonial empires in Asia and Africa. The British Empire was at its zenith; the United States was expanding westward; and the Ottoman Empire was in visible decline. This was the age of Romanticism, early socialism, and the rise of mass politics.

events.title: Battle of the Alamo
events.description: A 13-day siege of the Alamo mission in San Antonio, Texas, by Mexican forces under General Antonio López de Santa Anna against Texian (American settler) defenders. The Mexican army stormed the compound on 6 March 1836, killing all defenders including James Bowie and Davy Crockett. Though a tactical Mexican victory, the siege galvanized Texian resistance and became a symbolic rallying point for Texas independence. The battle occurred during the Texas Revolution (1835–1836), which sought to separate Texas from Mexico.
key_figures: Antonio López de Santa Anna (Mexican general)

sources.name: Mexican military records and Santa Anna's correspondence
Texian survivor accounts and later Texas historiography
Archaeological excavations at the Alamo site

events.certainty_note: The siege dates and basic facts are well-documented in contemporary Mexican military records and Texian accounts. The exact number of defenders and some tactical details remain debated, but the core event is firmly established.
cross_references: 1836-002 (Battle of San Jacinto, consequence of Alamo)

events.title: Battle of San Jacinto
events.description: On 21 April 1836, Texian forces under General Sam Houston defeated Mexican forces under Santa Anna near the San Jacinto River, east of present-day Houston. The battle lasted approximately 18 minutes and resulted in a decisive Texian victory, with heavy Mexican casualties and the capture of Santa Anna. This victory effectively secured Texian independence and led to the establishment of the Republic of Texas. Santa Anna, under duress, signed the Treaty of Velasco recognizing Texian independence, though Mexico never formally ratified it.
key_figures: Antonio López de Santa Anna (Mexican commander, captured)

sources.name: Sam Houston's official reports and correspondence
Mexican military records and Santa Anna's later accounts

events.certainty_note: The date, location, and outcome are well-documented in contemporary sources from both sides. The exact casualty figures and some tactical details show minor variation across sources, but the core facts are secure.

events.title: Establishment of the Republic of Texas
events.description: Following the Texian victory at San Jacinto, the Republic of Texas was formally established as an independent nation. A constitutional convention met in March 1836 and drafted a constitution modeled on the U.S. Constitution. Sam Houston was elected the first president in September 1836. The republic maintained slavery and sought eventual annexation by the United States, which occurred in 1845. The republic's existence (1836–1845) was marked by financial instability, ongoing conflict with Mexico, and disputes with Native American tribes.
events.certainty_note: The establishment of the republic, its constitution, and Houston's election are all documented in contemporary official records and newspapers.

events.title: Accession of Queen Victoria
events.description: On 20 June 1836, William IV of the United Kingdom died, and his 18-year-old niece Victoria ascended to the throne. Victoria's accession marked the beginning of the Victorian era (1837–1901), one of the most significant periods in British history. Her reign witnessed the height of the British Empire, the Industrial Revolution's maturation, and profound social and cultural change. The accession also ended the personal union with Hanover, as Hanoverian law did not permit female succession, and the crown passed to Victoria's uncle, the Duke of Cumberland.
key_figures: Lord Melbourne (first prime minister under Victoria)
events.certainty_note: The date and circumstances of Victoria's accession are meticulously documented in official records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Indian Removal Act Consequences: Trail of Tears Begins
events.region: Southeastern United States and Indian Territory (present-day Oklahoma)
events.description: The forced removal of Native American nations from their ancestral lands in the Southeast to Indian Territory west of the Mississippi River, mandated by the Indian Removal Act of 1830, reached a critical phase in 1836. The Cherokee, Creek, Chickasaw, Choctaw, and Seminole nations were forcibly relocated. The Cherokee removal, known as the Trail of Tears, began in earnest in 1838–1839, but the process of forced displacement and death from disease, starvation, and exposure was well underway by 1836. Thousands died during the journey. This represents one of the darkest chapters in U.S. history and a catastrophic demographic collapse for Native American populations.
key_figures: Andrew Jackson (U.S. president, architect of removal policy)
John Ross (Cherokee principal chief, resisted removal)
Martin Van Buren (vice president, later president)

sources.name: U.S. government correspondence and military records
Cherokee and other Native American oral traditions and later written accounts
Modern historical scholarship and demographic analysis

events.certainty_note: The removal policy and its devastating consequences are well-documented in government records, contemporary accounts, and modern scholarship. The exact death toll remains debated, but the scale of the tragedy is undisputed.

events.title: Boer Great Trek Continues
events.description: The Great Trek, a mass migration of Boer (Dutch-descended) settlers from the Cape Colony into the interior of South Africa, continued throughout 1836. Motivated by British colonial policies, including the abolition of slavery in 1833 and the imposition of English law and language, thousands of Boers migrated northward and eastward. By 1836, several Boer groups had established settlements in what would become the Transvaal and Natal. This migration had profound consequences for indigenous African populations, leading to conflict, displacement, and the eventual establishment of Boer republics. The trek is celebrated in Afrikaner historiography but represents a traumatic displacement for indigenous peoples.

sources.name: Zulu oral traditions and later accounts

events.certainty_note: The Great Trek is well-documented in Boer records and British colonial sources. The motivations and consequences are debated in modern scholarship, particularly regarding the impact on indigenous populations.

events.title: Tithe Commutation Act (Britain)
events.description: The Tithe Commutation Act of 1836 reformed the system of tithes (agricultural taxes paid to the Church of England) in England and Wales. The act replaced the traditional system of paying tithes in kind (agricultural produce) with a fixed monetary payment based on grain prices. This reform was intended to reduce rural unrest caused by tithe disputes and to modernize agricultural taxation. The act had significant economic and social consequences, affecting the relationship between farmers, landowners, and the Church, and contributed to the decline of the Church's economic power in rural areas.
events.certainty_note: The act and its passage are documented in parliamentary records and legislation. Its economic and social effects are analyzed in modern historical scholarship.
cross_references: 1834-001 (Poor Law Amendment Act, related social reform)

events.title: Dickens' 'The Pickwick Papers' Published
events.description: Charles Dickens' first novel, 'The Pickwick Papers,' was published in serial form beginning in March 1836 and completed in November 1836. The novel was an immediate popular success and established Dickens as a major literary figure. It depicted English society with humor and social commentary, and its serialization in a magazine format helped establish the Victorian serial novel as a dominant literary form. The work's success launched Dickens' prolific career and his role as a chronicler of Victorian society and social problems.
events.certainty_note: The publication date and immediate success are well-documented in contemporary sources and literary records.

events.title: Siege of Missolonghi Aftermath and Greek Independence Consolidation
events.description: By 1836, the Greek War of Independence (1821–1829) had concluded with Greek independence recognized by the Treaty of Adrianople (1829). In 1836, Greece was consolidating its new status as an independent nation-state. The Kingdom of Greece was formally established in 1833 under King Otto of Bavaria. By 1836, the new Greek state was organizing its government, military, and economy, though it faced significant challenges including territorial disputes with the Ottoman Empire, economic instability, and internal political factionalism. The independence of Greece represented a major geopolitical shift in the Eastern Mediterranean and a symbolic victory for European nationalism and Philhellenism.
key_figures: King Otto of Bavaria (first king of Greece)
events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Greek independence and the establishment of the Kingdom of Greece are well-documented in diplomatic and governmental records.

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Daoguang Emperor's Reign Continues
events.description: In 1836, the Qing Dynasty under the Daoguang Emperor (r. 1820–1850) was experiencing significant internal and external pressures. The empire faced economic decline, administrative corruption, and the rise of secret societies and banditry. Externally, Western powers were increasingly demanding trade access, particularly Britain, which sought to expand opium trade into China. The Daoguang Emperor's reign would be marked by the First Opium War (1840–1842), which was being set up by escalating tensions in 1836. The period represents the beginning of the end of Qing dominance and the start of China's 'Century of Humiliation' at the hands of Western powers.
key_figures: British merchants and East India Company representatives

sources.name: Chinese historical chronicles and official histories

events.certainty_note: The political situation and tensions of 1836 are documented in Qing records and British sources. The broader context of decline and Western pressure is well-established in scholarship.

events.title: Boer-Zulu Conflict: Piet Retief's Mission
events.description: In 1836, Boer leader Piet Retief led a delegation to negotiate with Zulu King Dingane for land in Natal. Retief sought to establish a Boer settlement in the region. However, negotiations broke down, and in February 1837 (following 1836), Retief and his party were killed by Dingane's forces. This event sparked the Boer-Zulu War and led to the Battle of Blood River in December 1838. The conflict represents a crucial moment in South African history, marking the collision of Boer expansion with Zulu power and leading to the establishment of the Boer Republic of Natal.

sources.name: Zulu oral traditions and later accounts

events.certainty_note: The negotiations and Retief's mission in 1836 are documented in Boer sources, though the exact date of his death is sometimes given as February 1837. The broader context and consequences are well-established.

events.title: Indian Removal: Creek Nation Forced Westward
events.region: Southeastern United States to Indian Territory
events.description: The Creek Nation, one of the Five Civilized Tribes, was forcibly removed from their homeland in the Southeast to Indian Territory (present-day Oklahoma) in 1836–1837. The removal followed the Creek War (1813–1814) and the Indian Removal Act of 1830. Thousands of Creek people died during the forced march from disease, starvation, and harsh conditions. The removal represented a catastrophic demographic and cultural collapse for the Creek Nation and was part of the broader Indian Removal policy that displaced tens of thousands of Native Americans.

sources.name: U.S. government removal records and military correspondence
Creek oral traditions and later accounts

events.certainty_note: The removal of the Creek Nation in 1836–1837 is documented in U.S. government records and contemporary accounts. The death toll and suffering are well-established in modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Alamo has been contested by modern historians. While traditionally portrayed as a heroic last stand that galvanized Texian resistance, some scholars argue that the siege's military significance was limited and that the Texian victory at San Jacinto was more decisive in securing independence. Additionally, the death toll at the Alamo (approximately 183–257 defenders) has been debated, with some sources providing different figures. The narrative of the Alamo as a symbol of American heroism has been criticized as reflecting Anglo-American bias and minimizing the Mexican perspective and the broader context of the Texas Revolution as a conflict over slavery and territorial expansion. The Indian Removal Act's characterization as a 'necessary' policy has been thoroughly discredited by modern scholarship, which emphasizes its genocidal consequences and the agency of Native American resistance. The Great Trek is celebrated in Afrikaner historiography as a heroic migration but is increasingly understood in modern South African scholarship as a displacement of indigenous populations and a precursor to apartheid.
historiographic_note: The year 1836 is well-documented in Western sources, particularly regarding events in North America, Britain, and Europe. The documentation is rich for political, military, and cultural events in these regions. However, the perspective is heavily weighted toward European and American sources. The Indian Removal and its consequences are documented primarily from the U.S. government perspective, with Native American voices largely absent from contemporary written records and recovered mainly through oral tradition and modern scholarship. The Great Trek in South Africa is documented in Boer and British sources but less so from the perspective of indigenous African populations. The Qing Dynasty's internal situation in 1836 is documented in Chinese imperial records and British commercial correspondence, but the Chinese perspective is filtered through official historiography. The historiography of 1836 reflects the biases of the era: Western expansion is often portrayed as inevitable progress, while indigenous resistance is marginalized. Modern scholarship has worked to recover suppressed voices and reframe these events in terms of colonialism, displacement, and cultural destruction.

graph_edges.note: The Alamo siege galvanized Texian resistance and contributed to the conditions leading to the Battle of San Jacinto
The victory at San Jacinto enabled the establishment of the Republic of Texas
Victoria's accession in 1836 led to her coronation in 1837 and the beginning of the Victorian era
The Indian Removal Act consequences of 1836 led to the peak of the Trail of Tears in 1838–1839
The Great Trek of 1836 led to the Boer-Zulu conflict and the Battle of Blood River in 1838
The tensions and opium trade disputes of 1836 led to the First Opium War in 1840
Piet Retief's mission and death in 1836–1837 sparked the Boer-Zulu conflict leading to the Battle of Blood River
